title: Coherence? One Nation would bulldoze Australian foreign policy
gist: The 2026 Lowy Poll shows Australian support for cultural diversity falling from 90% (2024) to 73%, fuelling Pauline Hanson's One Nation surge amid speculation of a One Nation-Coalition deal. The authors argue One Nation's foreign policy is a collection of incoherent impulses — UN withdrawal, anti-migration, suspicion of regional neighbours, contradictory alliance loyalty plus self-reliance — and warn that in a 'vibes over policy' environment, coherence may no longer matter to voters.

insights.claim: The 2026 Lowy Poll shows Australian support for cultural diversity fell sharply from 90% in 2024 to 73% this year, a shift in national mood that helps explain the growing resonance of populist anti-establishment politics benefiting One Nation.
insights.speaker: Emily Foley
insights.so_what: A 17-point collapse in diversity support is a fast-moving sentiment indicator underpinning populist momentum; relevant to Australian political and policy-risk pricing.
insights.quote: Australian support for cultural diversity has fallen sharply from 90% in 2024 to 73% this year

insights.claim: Gina Rinehart gifting Hanson an orange bulldozer explicitly linked to Musk's chainsaw symbol of bureaucracy-cutting reveals how One Nation's populism is connected to a broader set of populist movements gaining traction across Western democracies.
insights.speaker: Emily Foley
insights.so_what: Elite backing (Rinehart) plus a global populist playbook signals One Nation is part of a transnational movement, not a fringe local outfit — raising its policy-influence ceiling.
insights.locator: When mining magnate Gina Rinehart recently gifted Hanson

insights.claim: Although media draw Hanson-Trump parallels, Australian populism operates differently — Australians do not necessarily like Trump, with trust in the US to act responsibly falling to the lowest level ever recorded under his administration — so Hanson borrows global right-wing rhetoric but has developed a distinctly Australian form of populism.
insights.speaker: Emily Foley
insights.so_what: Australian populism is not simply MAGA-imported; record-low US trust complicates any pro-Trump alignment, a nuance for forecasting Australian foreign-policy drift.
insights.quote: Hanson may borrow from global right-wing rhetoric... but she has developed a distinctly Australian form of populism

insights.claim: One Nation has committed to withdrawing Australia from the United Nations but, when pressed at the National Press Club on consequences for shipping rights, Hanson was evasive ('I'm not getting into that now'; 'let's see what happens at the time'), reflecting multilateral-institution scepticism common to right-wing populists.
insights.speaker: Emily Foley
insights.so_what: A UN-withdrawal commitment without worked-through consequences is a concrete policy-risk flag if One Nation gains governing influence; watch shipping/trade-rights exposure.
insights.quote: I'm not getting into that now
insights.locator: Hanson's address to the National Press Club

insights.claim: One Nation advocates lifting defence spending to 3.5% of GDP with heavier drone and missile investment and argues Australia cannot rely on the US, yet Hanson criticised the government for not doing more to support the Trump administration over the Strait of Hormuz conflict — prompting a Liberal MP to call her 'MAGA first'.
insights.speaker: Emily Foley
insights.so_what: The 3.5%-of-GDP defence pledge is materially higher spending; the contradictory self-reliance-plus-MAGA stance signals policy incoherence that complicates strategic forecasting.
insights.locator: We do have some indication of One Nation's approach to military spending

insights.claim: Hanson's troubled relationship with regional neighbours — from her 1990s 'swamped by Asians' speech to current hostility toward Muslim migrants and criticism of Indian and Chinese migration — has foreign-policy consequences, with PNG's Foreign Minister condemning her labelling of Pacific leaders as corrupt as 'false and defamatory'.
insights.speaker: Emily Foley
insights.so_what: Rhetoric damaging Australia's regional reputation is a real diplomatic cost; relevant to Australia's Asia/Pacific engagement and trade-relationship stability if One Nation gains sway.
insights.locator: Hanson's comments at the Press Club labelling Pacific leaders

insights.claim: One Nation's international agenda is primarily a collection of impulses — institutional scepticism, anti-migration hostility, suspicion of neighbours, an uneasy alliance-loyalty/self-reliance mix, plus anti-aid and anti-'woke'/DEI positions — yet in an environment where 'vibes' matter more than policy detail, coherence may be beside the point.
insights.speaker: Emily Foley
insights.so_what: Policy incoherence is no longer an electoral liability; for many voters credibility of views simply doesn't matter — a structural shift that raises tail risk of populist-driven foreign-policy disruption.
insights.quote: whether those views amount to credible policy simply doesn't matter
insights.locator: Such ambiguities might have traditionally posed a problem
